old_claim.note: Advanced Metallurgy (Medieval) provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Paré's innovations came from campaign surgery and battlefield wound care, so military field medicine is the relevant historical lineage rather than general medieval metallurgy.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica places Pare's gunshot-wound method in Renaissance military surgery and describes his treatment of campaign firearm injuries and amputation practice.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source reviews Pare's surgical innovations as military and campaign wound-care practice, including firearm injuries and amputation.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The evidence points to battlefield medical practice as the lineage; it does not support a broad metallurgy prerequisite, so the replacement is historical_predecessor and partly contextual.

ontology_before: Surgical Advances (Pare) depended on Advanced Metallurgy (Medieval), implying tool or prosthesis materials were the central causal substrate.
ontology_after: Surgical Advances (Pare) depends on Military Field Medicine as the campaign-surgery lineage for gunshot-wound and amputation practice.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: surgical_advances_pare now depends on military_field_medicine and no longer directly depends on advanced_metallurgy_medieval.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to metal surgical instruments or prosthesis fabrication rather than Pare's wound treatment, ligature, amputation, and military surgery.
A stronger source showed medieval metallurgy directly enabled the specific Pare innovations more than battlefield medical practice did.
A dedicated Renaissance military surgery node were introduced and should replace the broader military_field_medicine prerequisite.
short_reason: Pare's evidence is battlefield surgical practice, not broad medieval metallurgy.
